vulnerabilities.name: Stale privileged accounts of departed contractors retained in Active Directory
vulnerabilities.description: A review found seventeen enabled Active Directory accounts belonging to contractors and integrators whose engagements ended over six months ago, several with Domain Admin or delegated OT-management rights. The lack of a deprovisioning trigger tied to contract end leaves orphaned privileged access available for misuse.

vulnerabilities.name: No logging or SIEM coverage of the OT environment
vulnerabilities.description: Security event logging and forwarding to the SIEM stops at the corporate firewall; SCADA servers, RTUs and engineering workstations generate no centrally collected logs. There is no detection capability for anomalous OT commands, failed logons or configuration changes, undermining NIS2 incident-detection obligations.

vulnerabilities.name: Hard-coded API keys committed to the internal GitLab repository
vulnerabilities.description: Source code for the in-house generation-forecasting service contains hard-coded API keys and a database connection string committed in plaintext across multiple historical commits. The secrets grant access to the production forecasting database and a third-party weather API, and remain retrievable in git history despite later removal.

vulnerabilities.name: Weak TLS configuration on the customer self-service portal
vulnerabilities.description: The web portal where corporate energy-purchase customers view consumption data still accepts TLS 1.0/1.1 and several weak cipher suites, and lacks HSTS. The configuration exposes session data to downgrade and interception attacks and falls short of the platform's stated transport-security baseline.

vulnerabilities.name: Unsegregated backup network allows ransomware to reach the backup repository
vulnerabilities.description: The Veeam backup repository resides on the same domain and routable subnet as production file servers, with backup service accounts holding broad write access. A ransomware actor reaching one server could encrypt or delete the backups, eliminating the recovery path and breaching the immutability principle of the backup policy.

vulnerabilities.name: Phishing-susceptible workforce with no simulation programme
vulnerabilities.description: Voltara has never run a phishing simulation campaign and security-awareness training is a once-a-year slideshow. Finance and procurement staff who handle supplier payment changes have no practical exposure to business-email-compromise tactics, leaving the organization measurably vulnerable to invoice-fraud and credential-harvesting attacks.

vulnerabilities.name: Insecure firmware update mechanism on remote terminal units
vulnerabilities.description: Remote terminal units at unmanned wind and solar sites accept firmware updates over the network without cryptographic signature verification. A man-in-the-middle on the maintenance channel could push tampered firmware, granting persistent control over disconnection and protection relay logic.

vulnerabilities.name: Excessive Microsoft 365 global administrator assignments
vulnerabilities.description: Eleven users hold the Global Administrator role in the Microsoft 365 tenant, including several outside the IT team, and none use privileged-access management or just-in-time elevation. The broad standing privilege widens the blast radius of any single account compromise across mail, SharePoint and identity.

vulnerabilities.name: Unmanaged personal devices accessing corporate email without MDM
vulnerabilities.description: Field staff and managers sync corporate mailboxes to personal phones and tablets with no mobile device management, conditional access or remote-wipe capability. Lost or compromised personal devices retain corporate and personal data with no enforced encryption or PIN policy, creating both security and GDPR exposure.

vulnerabilities.name: No formal vulnerability management or patch SLA for IT systems
vulnerabilities.description: There is no documented patch-management process, vulnerability-scan cadence or remediation SLA for corporate servers and endpoints. Critical operating-system and application patches are applied ad hoc when an engineer has time, so high-severity vulnerabilities can remain live for many weeks without tracking.

vulnerabilities.name: Third-party O&M contractor with unrestricted standing remote access
vulnerabilities.description: The wind-turbine original-equipment manufacturer maintains a permanent always-on remote-access tunnel into the turbine control network for predictive maintenance, with no time-boxing, session recording or per-access approval. Voltara cannot see what the vendor does, and a compromise of the vendor would directly expose the generation fleet.

vulnerabilities.name: Plaintext SCADA operator passwords stored in HMI project files
vulnerabilities.description: HMI configuration project files saved on engineering workstations store operator authentication credentials in plaintext or trivially reversible encoding within the project XML. Anyone able to copy a project file - including backups taken offsite - can recover operator passwords for the control interface.

vulnerabilities.name: Unrestricted USB ports on engineering workstations at all sites
vulnerabilities.description: Engineering and operator workstations across wind, solar and hydro sites permit any USB mass-storage device with no device-control policy or scanning. Contractors routinely use personal USB sticks to transfer configuration files, a primary vector for malware bridging the air-gapped illusion into OT.

vulnerabilities.name: Inadequate SQL injection protection on the legacy maintenance ticketing app
vulnerabilities.description: An in-house maintenance ticketing web application built years ago concatenates user input directly into SQL queries on several search and filter endpoints. The application is reachable on the internal network and holds asset and personnel data, making it exploitable for database extraction by any authenticated user.

vulnerabilities.name: Missing security clauses in supplier and subcontractor contracts
vulnerabilities.description: A sample of contracts with IT and OT suppliers showed that fewer than half contain information-security, breach-notification or audit-right clauses. Without contractual obligations, Voltara cannot enforce its security baseline on the supply chain or demonstrate NIS2 supply-chain due diligence.

vulnerabilities.name: No tested business continuity or disaster recovery plan for the dispatch centre
vulnerabilities.description: The Lyon dispatch and monitoring centre has an outdated business-continuity document that has never been exercised, and no validated failover to the secondary site. Recovery-time and recovery-point objectives are undefined, so an outage of the primary centre would have an unknown and likely prolonged impact on operations oversight.

vulnerabilities.name: Weak password policy permitting eight-character passwords without complexity
vulnerabilities.description: The Active Directory password policy allows eight-character passwords with no complexity or breached-password screening and a 365-day maximum age. The weak baseline, combined with single-factor logon on several internal systems, makes brute-force and password-spraying attacks practical.

vulnerabilities.name: Unencrypted laptops issued to field engineers
vulnerabilities.description: A portion of the field-engineering laptop fleet was deployed before BitLocker was mandated and remains without full-disk encryption. These devices carry site network diagrams, VPN profiles and personal data, so a theft or loss directly exposes sensitive material and triggers GDPR breach assessment.

vulnerabilities.name: Default SNMP community strings on network switches and routers
vulnerabilities.description: Site and corporate network switches and routers respond to SNMP queries using the default public and private community strings over SNMPv1/v2c. An attacker on the network can enumerate topology and, via the writable string, alter device configuration without authentication.

vulnerabilities.name: No data retention or deletion schedule for personal data
vulnerabilities.description: Voltara has no documented retention schedule or automated deletion process for personal data held in HR, CRM and CCTV systems. Records of former employees, job applicants and visitor logs are retained indefinitely, breaching the GDPR storage-limitation principle and inflating breach exposure.

vulnerabilities.name: Publicly accessible Grafana monitoring dashboard without authentication
vulnerabilities.description: A Grafana instance visualizing real-time generation and grid-export figures was exposed to the internet for a vendor demo and left running with anonymous viewer access enabled. It leaks commercially sensitive operational data and reveals system internals useful for reconnaissance.

vulnerabilities.name: Lack of network access control for visitor and contractor connections
vulnerabilities.description: Office and site network ports and Wi-Fi accept any device without 802.1X or captive-portal authentication. Visitors and contractors plug directly into the corporate LAN, providing an unmonitored foothold and bypassing the assumption that physical presence is the only control.

vulnerabilities.name: Inverter cybersecurity certificates expired and not renewed
vulnerabilities.description: TLS certificates securing the management interfaces of several solar inverter clusters have expired, and operators have trained themselves to click through the browser warnings. The normalized warning-bypass behaviour defeats certificate validation and conceals any genuine man-in-the-middle attempt.

vulnerabilities.name: No segregation of duties in the financial payment process
vulnerabilities.description: In the small finance team a single accountant can create a supplier, change its bank details and release the payment without a second approver. The absence of dual control over payment changes is a direct enabler of internal fraud and business-email-compromise payment diversion.

vulnerabilities.name: Cleartext credentials transmitted by the legacy weather-data feed integration
vulnerabilities.description: The integration pulling meteorological forecast data for generation planning authenticates over plain HTTP with credentials in the URL query string. The credentials traverse the network and are written to proxy and server logs in cleartext, exposing the third-party account.

vulnerabilities.name: Absent incident response plan and undefined NIS2 reporting workflow
vulnerabilities.description: Voltara has no documented incident-response plan, no defined roles, and no procedure for the NIS2 24-hour early-warning and 72-hour notification timeline to the national CSIRT. Staff would not know whom to alert or how to classify a significant incident, risking both prolonged impact and regulatory non-compliance.

vulnerabilities.name: Shared service account running scheduled jobs with domain admin rights
vulnerabilities.description: A single service account configured with Domain Admin privileges runs dozens of scheduled tasks and application services across multiple servers, with its password stored in scripts and unchanged for years. Compromise of any host running this account yields full domain control.

vulnerabilities.name: Unmonitored physical access to unmanned generation sites
vulnerabilities.description: Several remote wind and solar sites rely on a padlocked fence and a single keyed cabinet, with no intrusion detection, no CCTV upload and no tamper alerting on control cabinets. Physical intrusion to manipulate RTUs or protection relays could go undetected for days between site visits.

vulnerabilities.name: DNS resolver permits open recursion and lacks DNSSEC validation
vulnerabilities.description: The internal DNS infrastructure allows recursive queries from broad source ranges and does not validate DNSSEC. The configuration exposes the resolver to cache-poisoning and amplification abuse, and lets spoofed responses redirect internal clients to attacker-controlled hosts.

vulnerabilities.name: No malware protection on Linux-based historian and OPC servers
vulnerabilities.description: Linux servers running the process historian and OPC gateway have no endpoint protection or integrity monitoring deployed, on the assumption that Linux is low risk. Without file-integrity or anti-malware controls, tampering or implants on these critical data-aggregation hosts would not be detected.

vulnerabilities.name: Wi-Fi at the Lyon headquarters using a shared pre-shared key
vulnerabilities.description: Corporate wireless at headquarters uses WPA2-PSK with a single pre-shared key distributed widely and never rotated, even after staff departures. Anyone who has ever held the key, or who captures and cracks the handshake, retains persistent access to the internal network.

vulnerabilities.name: Improper access rights review for the HR and payroll system
vulnerabilities.description: Access to the HR and payroll application has never undergone a formal periodic recertification. Managers who changed roles retain access to salary and personal data of teams they no longer lead, breaching least-privilege and GDPR data-minimization expectations.

vulnerabilities.name: Verbose error pages disclosing stack traces on the public website
vulnerabilities.description: The corporate marketing website returns detailed framework stack traces and server version banners when an unhandled error occurs. The information disclosure aids attacker reconnaissance by revealing software versions, file paths and library details that should be suppressed in production.

vulnerabilities.name: No multi-factor authentication on the cloud GRC platform admin accounts
vulnerabilities.description: Administrator accounts for the cloud-hosted GRC platform itself authenticate with password only. Given that the platform aggregates the full risk register, asset inventory and audit evidence, a single compromised admin credential would expose the organization's entire security posture documentation.

vulnerabilities.name: Inconsistent timezone and clock synchronization across logging systems
vulnerabilities.description: Servers and network devices are not consistently synchronized to a common NTP source and record events in mixed local and UTC times. The drift and inconsistency make cross-system correlation during incident investigation unreliable and could undermine the evidential value of logs.

vulnerabilities.name: Sensitive documents shared via personal cloud file-sharing accounts
vulnerabilities.description: Project teams routinely use personal Dropbox and WeTransfer accounts to exchange grid-connection studies and contracts with external partners because the sanctioned platform is cumbersome. The shadow-IT practice moves confidential and personal data outside corporate control with no logging, retention or access governance.

vulnerabilities.name: Protection relay settings modifiable without change control
vulnerabilities.description: Engineers can alter protection-relay setpoints at substations directly via the relay front panel or local laptop with no change-control workflow, approval or recorded justification. Unauthorized or erroneous setting changes could disable grid-fault protection or cause unwarranted disconnections, with no audit trail to detect them.

vulnerabilities.name: Outdated and unsupported Cisco ASA firewalls at perimeter
vulnerabilities.description: The perimeter firewalls at two sites are Cisco ASA models that have passed end of software maintenance and no longer receive vulnerability fixes. Known exploitable flaws in the VPN and management interfaces remain unpatchable, undermining the primary network boundary control.

vulnerabilities.name: No encryption of personal data at rest in the CRM database
vulnerabilities.description: The customer relationship management database storing corporate-client contacts, contract terms and billing details holds all personal data unencrypted at rest, including on backups. A database file or backup-media exfiltration would directly expose personal data with no cryptographic protection layer.

vulnerabilities.name: Lack of code review and security testing in the software delivery pipeline
vulnerabilities.description: In-house developed tools, including the forecasting and reporting services, are deployed without mandatory peer code review, dependency scanning or static analysis. Vulnerable third-party libraries and insecure code reach production undetected, and there is no record of secure-development assurance.

vulnerabilities.name: Excessive data collection in the visitor management system
vulnerabilities.description: The headquarters visitor sign-in tablet collects full identity-document scans and vehicle details for every visitor and retains them indefinitely in a local database. The collection exceeds what is necessary for the stated purpose, breaching GDPR data-minimization and creating an unjustified personal-data store.

vulnerabilities.name: Misconfigured email authentication allowing domain spoofing
vulnerabilities.description: The voltara-energy domain publishes an SPF record set to softfail and has no DMARC policy enforced. Attackers can spoof the company domain in phishing and invoice-fraud campaigns against staff, partners and customers without the messages being reliably rejected.

vulnerabilities.name: Unrestricted outbound internet access from OT engineering workstations
vulnerabilities.description: Engineering workstations within the control network can browse the internet and download files with no proxy filtering or egress restriction. The unrestricted outbound path enables drive-by malware delivery and would provide a ready command-and-control channel for any implant on the OT side.

vulnerabilities.name: No formal asset inventory for OT devices and firmware versions
vulnerabilities.description: There is no maintained inventory of operational-technology assets, their firmware versions or network locations across the generation fleet. Without it, Voltara cannot assess which devices are affected by a newly disclosed vulnerability or verify patch status, undermining vulnerability and incident management.

vulnerabilities.name: Backup media stored offsite without encryption or chain of custody
vulnerabilities.description: Monthly backup tapes are couriered to a manager's home for offsite retention, unencrypted and without any logged chain of custody. The arrangement risks loss or theft of complete data sets including personal data, with no protection and no ability to prove the media's integrity.

vulnerabilities.name: Privilege escalation via writable service binary paths on dispatch servers
vulnerabilities.description: Several Windows services on the dispatch application servers run from directories where standard users hold write permission, and some service paths are unquoted. A low-privileged user or malware could replace the executable to escalate to SYSTEM, compromising a critical operational host.

vulnerabilities.name: Reliance on a single key administrator with no documented procedures
vulnerabilities.description: Detailed knowledge of the SCADA configuration, network topology and recovery steps resides almost entirely with one long-serving administrator, with little documentation. This single point of human failure means an unavailability of that individual would severely impair Voltara's ability to operate and recover its systems.

risks.name: Ransomware encrypts SCADA control servers across wind farm portfolio
risks.description: A ransomware strain laterally moves from corporate IT into the OT network and encrypts the SCADA control servers managing the onshore wind fleet, halting remote turbine control.

risks.name: Compromised remote-access VPN to substation HMI workstations
risks.description: Stolen VPN credentials let an attacker reach the human-machine interface workstations at grid substations and manipulate breaker and protection settings.

risks.name: Tampering with solar inverter firmware via vendor update channel
risks.description: An attacker poisons the inverter manufacturer's firmware update server, pushing malicious firmware that destabilizes power output across multiple solar parks.

risks.name: GPS time-source spoofing disrupts synchrophasor measurements
risks.description: Spoofed GPS timing signals desynchronize phasor measurement units, corrupting grid-stability telemetry and triggering erroneous protection trips.

risks.name: Insider exfiltration of grid-connection and capacity bidding data
risks.description: A disgruntled trading-desk employee copies confidential day-ahead capacity bids and grid-connection studies before resigning to a competitor.

risks.name: Phishing harvests Office 365 credentials of finance team
risks.description: A targeted phishing campaign captures Microsoft 365 credentials of accounts-payable staff, enabling mailbox access and fraudulent invoice redirection.

risks.name: Business email compromise reroutes turbine supplier payments
risks.description: An attacker impersonates a turbine OEM via a spoofed thread and convinces finance to wire a milestone payment to a fraudulent bank account.

risks.name: Unpatched VPN appliance exploited for initial foothold
risks.description: A known CVE in the perimeter VPN concentrator is exploited before patching, giving an attacker an authenticated foothold into the corporate network.

risks.name: Theft of unencrypted field engineer laptop with site schematics
risks.description: A field engineer's laptop containing single-line diagrams and site network maps is stolen from a vehicle, exposing sensitive OT design data.

risks.name: Misconfigured S3 bucket exposes SCADA historian backups
risks.description: A cloud storage bucket holding process-historian database exports is left publicly readable, leaking years of operational and metering data.

risks.name: DDoS attack saturates customer self-service portal
risks.description: A volumetric distributed denial-of-service attack overwhelms the consumer energy portal, blocking customers from viewing usage and submitting meter readings.

risks.name: Malicious USB introduces malware to air-gapped hydro plant
risks.description: A contractor's infected USB stick used for commissioning carries malware into the isolated control network of a run-of-river hydro plant.

risks.name: Third-party maintenance contractor over-privileged remote session
risks.description: A turbine maintenance vendor retains standing administrative remote access well beyond the service window, widening the attack surface on OT assets.

risks.name: Smart-meter data breach exposes household consumption profiles
risks.description: A flaw in the metering data management system leaks granular half-hourly household consumption profiles, a GDPR personal-data breach.

risks.name: Weak segmentation lets IT malware reach OT DMZ
risks.description: Flat network design allows a commodity worm from the office network to traverse into the operational technology demilitarized zone.

risks.name: Default credentials on remote terminal units left unchanged
risks.description: Substation remote terminal units retain factory default passwords, allowing trivial unauthorized configuration changes by anyone reaching them.

risks.name: Supply-chain compromise of asset-monitoring SaaS vendor
risks.description: A breach at the cloud condition-monitoring provider exposes Voltara's turbine telemetry and grants attacker pivot access to integration APIs.

risks.name: Privileged Active Directory account compromise enables domain takeover
risks.description: An attacker escalates from a compromised workstation to a Domain Admin account, gaining control over the entire corporate identity infrastructure.

risks.name: Sensitive personal data of employees leaked via HR portal flaw
risks.description: An access-control bug in the HR self-service portal lets employees view colleagues' salary, health and bank details, breaching GDPR.

risks.name: Shared service account credentials hardcoded in automation scripts
risks.description: A service account password embedded in deployment scripts in a Git repository is discoverable and reusable across multiple systems.

risks.name: Spear-phishing of OT operators delivers remote access trojan
risks.description: Control-room operators receive a tailored lure that installs a remote access trojan on a workstation bridging IT and OT zones.

risks.name: Wind turbine controller denial-of-service via crafted Modbus packets
risks.description: Malformed Modbus/TCP packets crash the programmable logic controllers governing turbine pitch, forcing emergency shutdowns.

risks.name: Loss of encryption keys renders backup archives unrecoverable
risks.description: Mismanagement of the backup encryption key vault leaves disaster-recovery archives permanently undecryptable when restoration is needed.

risks.name: Cloud misconfiguration grants public access to GDPR data warehouse
risks.description: An overly permissive IAM role exposes the analytics data warehouse containing customer and employee personal data to anonymous access.

risks.name: Stolen API token abused to extract trading platform positions
risks.description: A leaked API token for the energy-trading platform lets an attacker read open positions and submit unauthorized market orders.

risks.name: Rogue wireless access point in substation control building
risks.description: An unauthorized Wi-Fi access point plugged into the substation LAN provides a covert bridge into the OT network from the parking lot.

risks.name: Outdated Windows XP HMIs vulnerable to known exploits
risks.description: Legacy unsupported operating systems on plant HMI panels expose them to widely available exploits with no available vendor patches.

risks.name: Misdelivered email sends customer PII to wrong recipient
risks.description: An auto-complete error sends a spreadsheet of customer contact and consumption data to an external party, triggering a GDPR notification.

risks.name: Compromise of CI/CD pipeline injects backdoor into metering app
risks.description: An attacker with access to the build pipeline inserts a backdoor into a release of the smart-metering mobile application.

risks.name: Vendor laptop introduces ransomware during turbine commissioning
risks.description: An OEM engineer connects an infected laptop to the plant network during commissioning, deploying ransomware to local control systems.

risks.name: Social engineering of helpdesk bypasses MFA reset controls
risks.description: An attacker convinces the IT helpdesk to reset multi-factor authentication on an executive account using pretexting.

risks.name: Exposed Modbus and DNP3 ports discoverable on internet scanners
risks.description: Internet-facing industrial protocol ports on a remote solar site appear in public scan databases, inviting opportunistic OT attacks.

risks.name: Privileged contractor retains access after contract termination
risks.description: Deprovisioning failure leaves a departed consultant's VPN and application accounts active and exploitable for months.

risks.name: Manipulation of energy-production figures for regulatory fraud
risks.description: An insider alters generation-output records in the reporting database to overstate renewable production for subsidy claims.

risks.name: Weak TLS configuration enables interception of telemetry traffic
risks.description: Obsolete cipher suites on the telemetry gateway allow an on-path attacker to decrypt and modify turbine performance data in transit.

risks.name: Lost mobile device with cached corporate email and documents
risks.description: An executive's unmanaged smartphone with cached confidential strategy documents is lost while travelling, with no remote wipe possible.

risks.name: Shadow IT cloud spreadsheet stores grid-asset inventory
risks.description: A team uses an unsanctioned consumer cloud account to maintain the critical-asset inventory, outside security monitoring and access control.

risks.name: Brute-force attack against externally exposed RDP server
risks.description: An internet-facing Remote Desktop service is brute-forced, giving an attacker an interactive session on a server in the corporate estate.

risks.name: Tampered protection-relay settings cause cascading grid trips
risks.description: Unauthorized changes to digital protection-relay thresholds at a substation produce nuisance trips that disconnect generation from the grid.

risks.name: Data poisoning of predictive-maintenance machine learning model
risks.description: Injected false sensor readings corrupt the maintenance prediction model, masking genuine turbine faults until catastrophic failure.

risks.name: Unauthorized disclosure of merger and acquisition due-diligence files
risks.description: A breach of the deal-room file share leaks confidential M&A due-diligence material on a planned hydro-asset acquisition.

risks.name: DNS hijacking redirects corporate domain to phishing clone
risks.description: Compromise of the domain registrar account redirects Voltara's web domain to a credential-harvesting clone of the employee login page.

risks.name: Insecure mobile field-service app stores credentials in plaintext
risks.description: The technician field app caches login tokens and site access codes unencrypted on the device, exposing them if the phone is compromised.

risks.name: Supply-chain firmware backdoor in imported battery storage controllers
risks.description: Battery energy storage system controllers from an overseas supplier contain an undocumented remote-access function enabling covert control.

risks.name: Accidental deletion of production database without recoverable backup
risks.description: A mistaken administrator command drops the operational reporting database and the most recent backup proves corrupt and unrestorable.

risks.name: Compromised certificate authority enables forged code signing
risks.description: Theft of the internal code-signing certificate lets an attacker sign and deploy malicious software trusted by OT and IT endpoints.

risks.name: Phishing-induced wire fraud impersonating the CFO
risks.description: A whaling attack spoofs the CFO and pressures treasury staff into an urgent unauthorized transfer for a fictitious acquisition deposit.

risks.name: Eavesdropping on unencrypted radio link to remote hydro telemetry
risks.description: An unencrypted point-to-point radio carrying remote hydro-plant telemetry is intercepted and spoofed by an attacker with RF equipment.

risks.name: Excessive data retention exposes obsolete customer records in breach
risks.description: Customer records kept far beyond their lawful retention period are compromised in a breach, amplifying GDPR exposure and fines.

risks.name: Compromise of managed security service provider monitoring access
risks.description: A breach at the outsourced SOC provider grants attackers privileged read access into Voltara's logging and detection infrastructure.

risks.name: Malicious insider plants logic bomb in dispatch scheduling system
risks.description: A developer embeds time-triggered destructive code in the generation-dispatch scheduler set to activate after their departure.

risks.name: Replay attack on substation command channel reissues breaker operations
risks.description: Lacking anti-replay protection, captured legitimate control commands are replayed to operate substation circuit breakers without authorization.

risks.name: Unauthorized scope creep of MCP integration exposes risk register
risks.description: An over-broadly scoped API integration token allows an external assistant to read the full confidential risk register and asset data.

risks.name: Watering-hole attack via compromised industry association website
risks.description: Employees visiting a trade-body portal are served drive-by malware after the association's site is compromised by a targeted actor.

risks.name: Drone-based RF capture of wireless sensor network at solar park
risks.description: A drone hovers over an unmanned solar park to capture and analyze unencrypted wireless sensor traffic, mapping site instrumentation.

risks.name: Credential stuffing breaches reused passwords on engineering portal
risks.description: Passwords reused from a third-party breach are stuffed against the engineering documentation portal, granting attacker access to design files.

risks.name: Misconfigured firewall rule exposes engineering jump host
risks.description: An overly permissive change leaves the OT jump host directly reachable from the internet, removing a key segmentation control.

risks.name: Loss of availability from expired TLS certificate on grid API
risks.description: An unrenewed certificate on the grid-operator data exchange API halts mandatory real-time generation reporting until replaced.

risks.name: Tampered metering data understates exported energy for settlement
risks.description: Manipulated export-metering records reduce reported energy delivered to the grid, distorting financial settlement with the network operator.

risks.name: Quishing campaign uses QR codes to bypass email link filtering
risks.description: Malicious QR codes in printed and emailed notices direct staff to a credential-harvesting site that evades URL-based defenses.

risks.name: Abandoned legacy server with unmonitored vulnerabilities
risks.description: A forgotten decommissioned-in-name-only server remains powered on with no patching or monitoring, offering a quiet foothold for attackers.

action_plans.name: SCADA Remote Access MFA Rollout for Wind Farm Controllers
action_plans.gap: Engineers connect to the Greoux and Cruas onshore wind park SCADA HMIs over the corporate VPN using shared single-factor credentials, with no multi-factor authentication enforced on the OT jump host. This violates the NIS2 access control baseline and ISO 27001 A.8.5 for privileged OT access.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy a hardened OT jump host with FIDO2 hardware-key MFA in front of all wind park SCADA HMIs, retire shared accounts in favour of named per-engineer identities federated to the OT IdP, and enforce session recording. Pilot at Greoux in Q3, extend to Cruas and the remaining five parks by year-end, and add quarterly access recertification.

action_plans.name: GDPR Lawful Basis Mapping for Smart-Meter Consumption Data
action_plans.gap: The residential solar self-consumption portal ingests half-hourly smart-meter readings tied to named customers, but the Article 30 record of processing lists no documented lawful basis and no retention period for this granular consumption data, breaching GDPR Articles 6 and 30.
action_plans.remediation: Run a DPIA on the smart-meter ingestion pipeline, document legitimate-interest versus contractual-necessity bases per data field, set a 36-month rolling retention with automated purge, update the RoPA, and publish a layered privacy notice in the customer portal. Validate with the DPO before go-live.

action_plans.name: NIS2 24-Hour Early-Warning Incident Reporting Procedure
action_plans.gap: Voltara has no documented procedure to submit the NIS2 early-warning notification to ANSSI/the French CSIRT within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident; the current playbook only references the obsolete voluntary reporting channel.
action_plans.remediation: Draft and approve a NIS2-aligned incident notification SOP with a 24h early-warning, 72h incident-notification and one-month final-report timeline, pre-fill notification templates, assign on-call duty officers, integrate the workflow into the ticketing tool, and run a tabletop exercise to validate the timeline against a simulated ransomware event.

action_plans.name: Backup Immutability for Hydro Plant Historian Databases
action_plans.gap: Backups of the Serre-Poncon and Vouglans hydro plant process historians are written to a network share reachable with the same domain credentials as production, leaving them deletable by a compromised admin account. There is no offline or immutable copy, contrary to ISO 27001 A.8.13.
action_plans.remediation: Implement immutable object-lock backups on a separate storage tier with WORM retention, add an air-gapped weekly copy held off-site, segregate backup service accounts from production domain credentials, and conduct a quarterly restore test of a full historian database to verify recoverability within the 8-hour RTO.

action_plans.name: Asset Inventory Completeness for Substation IEDs
action_plans.gap: The CMDB does not capture the intelligent electronic devices (protection relays, RTUs) installed across the 32 grid-connection substations; field audits found 140+ unrecorded networked devices, breaching ISO 27001 A.5.9 inventory of associated assets.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy a passive OT asset-discovery sensor on each substation network segment, reconcile discovered IEDs against the CMDB, assign each an owner and criticality rating, and establish a change-controlled onboarding gate so no new field device is energised without a CMDB record. Target 100% coverage within two reporting quarters.

action_plans.name: Third-Party Turbine Maintenance Vendor Security Clauses
action_plans.gap: The framework maintenance contracts with the turbine OEM and two regional service contractors contain no information-security or breach-notification clauses, despite these vendors holding remote diagnostic access to turbine controllers. This is a supplier-relationship gap under ISO 27001 A.5.19/A.5.20 and NIS2 supply-chain obligations.
action_plans.remediation: Introduce a standard security annex (access control, breach notification within 24h, right to audit, sub-processor disclosure, secure remote-access requirements) into all OT maintenance contracts, renegotiate the three existing agreements at renewal, and add a pre-contract security questionnaire to procurement for all OT vendors.

action_plans.name: Privileged Access Review for Energy Trading Platform
action_plans.gap: Access to the day-ahead and intraday power trading platform is never recertified; a leavers analysis found three former traders and one contractor still holding active accounts months after departure, violating ISO 27001 A.5.18 and the joiners/movers/leavers process.
action_plans.remediation: Integrate the trading platform with the HR-driven identity lifecycle so terminations auto-disable accounts within 4 hours, institute a quarterly manager-attestation access review, immediately revoke the four stale accounts, and add SoD checks to prevent a single trader holding both submission and approval rights.

action_plans.name: Encryption of Personal Data on Field Engineer Laptops
action_plans.gap: A sample of field engineers' laptops used at remote solar sites were found without full-disk encryption enabled, exposing stored landowner contact details and grid-access personal data if a device is lost or stolen, contrary to GDPR Article 32 and ISO 27001 A.8.24.
action_plans.remediation: Enforce BitLocker/LUKS full-disk encryption via MDM policy with TPM-backed keys and escrowed recovery, block network access for non-compliant endpoints through device-compliance conditional access, and add encryption status to the monthly endpoint-hygiene KPI reported to the ISMS steering committee.

action_plans.name: Network Segmentation Between Corporate IT and Wind OT
action_plans.gap: Penetration testing demonstrated a flat path from the corporate Lyon HQ LAN into the wind-park OT VLAN with no firewall inspection, meaning a phishing compromise on the office network could reach turbine controllers. This breaches the IEC 62443 zone/conduit model expected under NIS2.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy an industrial DMZ with a data-diode-protected historian replica and a stateful OT firewall enforcing deny-by-default conduit rules between IT and OT zones, migrate all IT-to-OT data exchange through brokered services, and validate segmentation with a follow-up penetration test before closing the finding.

action_plans.name: Logging and Monitoring Coverage for Cloud Tenant
action_plans.gap: The Azure tenant hosting the customer portal and HR systems has diagnostic logging disabled on several key resources, and no logs are forwarded to a central SIEM, so security events would go undetected, contravening ISO 27001 A.8.15/A.8.16.
action_plans.remediation: Enable resource-level diagnostic logging tenant-wide via Azure Policy, forward all sign-in, control-plane and key-vault logs to the central SIEM with 13-month retention, build detection rules for impossible-travel and mass-download patterns, and define a monitored use-case backlog reviewed monthly by the SOC.

action_plans.name: Data Subject Access Request Handling Workflow
action_plans.gap: Voltara has no tracked workflow for GDPR data-subject requests; two access requests from former employees were handled informally by email and one exceeded the one-month statutory deadline, breaching GDPR Articles 12 and 15.
action_plans.remediation: Implement a DSAR case-management workflow with intake form, identity verification, automated 30-day SLA timers with escalation, a data-source checklist covering HR, payroll, CCTV and the customer portal, and a quarterly metrics report to the DPO. Train the privacy and HR teams on the new process.

action_plans.name: Removable Media Control on OT Maintenance Workstations
action_plans.gap: USB ports on maintenance laptops carried between solar and wind sites are unrestricted, and no malware scanning kiosk exists; this is the most common OT infection vector and is uncontrolled, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.7 and A.7.10.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy USB device-control policy allowing only registered encrypted media, install a malware-scanning kiosk at each operations base that all inbound media must pass through, log all media usage on OT endpoints, and issue sanctioned cross-site transfer drives with quarterly reissue.

action_plans.name: Business Continuity Plan for the Lyon Network Operations Centre
action_plans.gap: The single Lyon NOC that monitors and dispatches all generation assets has no documented failover to an alternate site; a fire or extended power loss would blind operations, and no BCP scenario covers this single point of failure, contravening ISO 27001 A.5.29/A.5.30.
action_plans.remediation: Establish a warm standby NOC at the Toulouse office with replicated monitoring tooling and pre-provisioned operator workstations, document and approve a NOC failover runbook with a 2-hour activation target, and validate it through an annual full failover exercise reported to the management review.

action_plans.name: Vulnerability Patch Management SLA for Internet-Facing Systems
action_plans.gap: An external scan found the customer portal and remote-access gateway running components with critical CVEs more than 90 days old; there is no defined remediation SLA tied to vulnerability severity, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.8.
action_plans.remediation: Adopt a risk-based patch SLA (critical internet-facing 7 days, high 30 days, others 90 days), onboard all external assets into the authenticated vulnerability scanner, remediate the outstanding criticals immediately, and publish a monthly exposure-burndown KPI with overdue items escalated to the CISO.

action_plans.name: Security Awareness Training for Substation Field Staff
action_plans.gap: Annual security-awareness completion sits at 41% among field and substation operations staff because the e-learning is desktop-only and these workers rarely use a PC; the training obligation under ISO 27001 A.6.3 is effectively unmet for the highest-risk OT population.
action_plans.remediation: Procure mobile-friendly microlearning delivered via the field workforce app, run quarterly toolbox-talk briefings on OT-specific threats (USB, tailgating, vishing), set a 95% completion target tracked per team, and tie completion to the supervisor performance dashboard.

action_plans.name: Cryptographic Key Management Policy for TLS Certificates
action_plans.gap: There is no inventory or lifecycle process for the TLS certificates protecting the customer portal, trading platform and API gateway; an undocumented certificate expired and caused a 4-hour outage, indicating a key-management control gap under ISO 27001 A.8.24.
action_plans.remediation: Stand up an automated certificate-management platform (ACME-based) with a complete certificate inventory, 30-day expiry alerting, enforced key strength and CA policy, and auto-renewal for eligible endpoints. Document a key-management policy covering generation, storage in the HSM-backed vault, rotation and revocation.

action_plans.name: Remote-Access Auditing for Inverter Manufacturer Support
action_plans.gap: The solar inverter manufacturer maintains a permanent always-on cellular modem inside each utility-scale plant for remote support, with no Voltara visibility, approval workflow or session logging over their access, breaching NIS2 supply-chain and ISO 27001 A.8.21 controls.
action_plans.remediation: Replace vendor-owned permanent modems with a Voltara-brokered remote-access platform requiring per-session approval, time-boxed access windows, full session recording and an automatic disconnect, contractually prohibit unsanctioned back-channels, and audit cellular interfaces at each plant during the next maintenance cycle.

action_plans.name: ISMS Internal Audit Programme Coverage
action_plans.gap: The internal audit programme has not covered the OT environment, supplier management or the EBIOS RM risk process in the last two cycles; ISO 27001 Clause 9.2 requires planned audits covering all of the ISMS, and the current plan leaves material scope unaudited.
action_plans.remediation: Revise the three-year rolling internal-audit schedule to ensure every Annex A theme, all clauses and the OT scope are audited at least once per cycle with risk-weighted frequency, train two additional internal auditors with OT competence, and report coverage and findings to each management review.

action_plans.name: Records Retention and Disposal for Decommissioned HR Files
action_plans.gap: Personnel files of leavers, including health and disciplinary records, are retained indefinitely in a shared drive with no disposal schedule, exceeding GDPR storage-limitation requirements (Article 5(1)(e)) and lacking the records-management control of ISO 27001 A.5.33.
action_plans.remediation: Define and approve an HR records-retention schedule aligned to French labour and social-security law, implement automated tagging and time-based disposal in the document store, securely purge records beyond their retention window after legal review, and add an annual retention-compliance check to the privacy programme.

action_plans.name: Physical Access Control at Unmanned Solar Substations
action_plans.gap: Several unmanned utility-scale solar substations rely on a single padlock with widely-copied keys and have no intrusion detection or access logging; an unauthorised entry at the Narbonne site went unnoticed for days, breaching ISO 27001 A.7.1/A.7.2.
action_plans.remediation: Upgrade perimeter and control-room doors to logged electronic access control with PIN/badge, install monitored intrusion detection and CCTV linked to the NOC, replace mechanical master keys with a managed keying scheme, and integrate alarms into the central monitoring and incident process.

action_plans.name: Supplier Sub-Processor Disclosure for Cloud Payroll
action_plans.gap: The outsourced payroll provider transfers employee data to undisclosed sub-processors, some potentially outside the EEA, with no documented transfer mechanism or sub-processor list, breaching GDPR Articles 28 and 44-46.
action_plans.remediation: Obtain a complete sub-processor list and data-flow map from the payroll provider, execute updated Standard Contractual Clauses with a transfer impact assessment for any non-EEA flow, require 30-day advance notice of sub-processor changes, and add the provider to the annual third-party privacy audit.

action_plans.name: Change Management for OT Firmware Updates
action_plans.gap: Turbine and inverter firmware updates are applied by vendors ad hoc with no change request, risk assessment, rollback plan or testing on a reference unit; an untested firmware push degraded output at two wind parks, showing a change-control gap under ISO 27001 A.8.32.
action_plans.remediation: Establish an OT change advisory board, mandate change requests with risk assessment and rollback plans for all firmware updates, require validation on a reference/staging unit before fleet rollout, schedule changes within maintenance windows, and retain a signed-off change record for each update.

action_plans.name: Mobile Device Management for BYOD Email Access
action_plans.gap: Employees access corporate email and SharePoint from personal phones with no MDM enrolment, PIN enforcement or remote-wipe capability, so a lost personal device could expose corporate and personal data, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.1 and GDPR Article 32.
action_plans.remediation: Roll out mobile application management with containerised corporate apps requiring device PIN, jailbreak/root detection and selective remote wipe, block unmanaged-device access via conditional access, publish an acceptable-use BYOD policy, and obtain employee acknowledgement before enabling access.

action_plans.name: Secure Configuration Baseline for Windows Servers
action_plans.gap: Servers are built manually from a 2019 gold image with no hardening standard; a configuration review found default services, weak local-admin passwords and disabled host firewalls across the fleet, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.9 secure configuration.
action_plans.remediation: Adopt the CIS Level 1 benchmark as the server hardening baseline enforced via configuration-as-code, rebuild the gold image, deploy a host-based firewall and LAPS for local-admin password rotation, and run automated weekly compliance scans with drift remediation reported in the monthly hardening KPI.

action_plans.name: Incident Response Plan Testing and Lessons Learned
action_plans.gap: The incident response plan has never been exercised and the last real incident produced no post-incident review or lessons-learned actions, leaving the plan untested and continuous improvement absent under ISO 27001 A.5.24/A.5.27.
action_plans.remediation: Run two scenario-based exercises per year (one ransomware, one OT-targeted) with documented after-action reports, formalise a post-incident review template feeding corrective actions into the ISMS, track lessons-learned actions to closure, and report exercise outcomes to the management review.

action_plans.name: Privacy Notice and Cookie Consent on Public Website
action_plans.gap: The voltara-energy public website sets analytics and marketing cookies before consent and the cookie banner offers no granular reject option, breaching the GDPR/ePrivacy consent requirements enforced by the CNIL.
action_plans.remediation: Implement a CNIL-compliant consent management platform that blocks non-essential cookies until explicit opt-in, provides equal accept/reject prominence and granular category control, logs consent records, and re-audit the site quarterly with the marketing team to catch newly added trackers.

action_plans.name: Segregation of Duties in the Finance ERP
action_plans.gap: In the ERP, several finance users can both create a supplier and approve and release payments to it, with no compensating control; an internal review flagged this as a fraud risk and a segregation-of-duties gap under ISO 27001 A.5.3.
action_plans.remediation: Define a SoD conflict matrix for the procure-to-pay and order-to-cash cycles, reconfigure ERP roles to eliminate toxic combinations, route unavoidable conflicts through documented compensating controls with secondary approval, and run a quarterly SoD violation report reviewed by internal audit.

action_plans.name: Data Classification Scheme Adoption Across Repositories
action_plans.gap: There is no information classification scheme in use; sensitive grid-interconnection drawings and commercial PPA contracts sit in open team folders alongside public material, breaching ISO 27001 A.5.12/A.5.13 labelling and handling controls.
action_plans.remediation: Define a four-tier classification scheme (Public/Internal/Confidential/Restricted) with handling rules, apply sensitivity labels in the document platform with auto-classification for key document types, restrict access to Confidential and Restricted material, and train staff with a short labelling guide and posters.

action_plans.name: NIS2 Governance and Management Accountability Evidence
action_plans.gap: NIS2 requires management bodies to approve cybersecurity risk-management measures and undergo training, but Voltara's board has no record of approving the ISMS risk treatment, and directors have received no cybersecurity training, leaving a governance accountability gap.
action_plans.remediation: Add a standing cybersecurity item to the board agenda, obtain documented board approval of the risk-treatment plan and the statement of applicability, deliver a tailored NIS2-accountability training session to the management body, and record approvals and attendance as audit evidence.

action_plans.name: Anti-Malware Coverage on Linux Application Servers
action_plans.gap: Endpoint protection is deployed only on Windows; the Linux servers hosting the customer portal, API gateway and trading microservices have no anti-malware or EDR agent, leaving a detection blind spot under ISO 27001 A.8.7.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy the EDR agent's Linux build across all in-scope Linux servers and containers, integrate alerts into the SOC workflow, enable file-integrity monitoring on key directories, and add agent-coverage percentage to the monthly endpoint KPI with a 100% target for production systems.

action_plans.name: Documented Information Control for ISMS Procedures
action_plans.gap: ISMS policies and procedures exist as uncontrolled Word files on personal drives with no version control, approval status or review dates; staff reference outdated copies, breaching ISO 27001 Clause 7.5 control of documented information.
action_plans.remediation: Migrate all ISMS documentation into a controlled repository with enforced versioning, defined owners, approval workflow, annual review cycles and read-acknowledgement tracking, withdraw obsolete copies, and publish a single authoritative policy index linked from the intranet.

action_plans.name: Capacity and Performance Monitoring for the Trading Platform
action_plans.gap: There is no proactive capacity monitoring for the trading platform; a month-end load spike exhausted database connections and caused a trading outage during the intraday session, indicating a capacity-management gap under ISO 27001 A.8.6.
action_plans.remediation: Implement resource and capacity monitoring with trend-based forecasting and alert thresholds for CPU, memory, connections and storage, define a capacity-review cadence ahead of seasonal load peaks, add autoscaling where supported, and document capacity plans for the next 12 months.

action_plans.name: Teleworking Security Policy and Home Network Guidance
action_plans.gap: A large share of HQ staff work hybrid, but there is no teleworking security policy covering home network security, screen privacy, secure disposal of printouts or split-tunnel VPN rules, leaving a gap against ISO 27001 A.6.7.
action_plans.remediation: Publish a teleworking security policy and supporting guidance, enforce full-tunnel VPN with device posture checks, provide privacy screens for staff handling confidential data, prohibit personal-device storage of corporate data, and include teleworking risks in the annual awareness campaign.

action_plans.name: Threat Intelligence Integration for the Energy Sector
action_plans.gap: The SOC consumes no sector-specific threat intelligence; it is unaware of active campaigns targeting European energy operators and ICS, so detection content is not tuned to relevant adversaries, a gap against ISO 27001 A.5.7 threat intelligence.
action_plans.remediation: Subscribe to the energy-ISAC and national CSIRT threat feeds, establish a process to translate relevant indicators and TTPs into SIEM detection rules within defined timelines, brief operations on emerging ICS threats monthly, and feed sector intelligence into the EBIOS RM risk-source analysis.

action_plans.name: Pseudonymisation of Personal Data in Analytics Environment
action_plans.gap: Production customer and employee personal data is copied verbatim into the analytics/BI environment for reporting, exposing identifiable data to a broad analyst population with no minimisation, breaching GDPR data-minimisation and Article 32 requirements.
action_plans.remediation: Introduce a pseudonymisation and masking layer in the analytics ingestion pipeline so direct identifiers are tokenised or removed, restrict re-identification keys to a small authorised group, document the lawful basis for analytics processing, and review BI dataset access against need-to-know quarterly.

action_plans.name: Wireless Network Security at Operations Bases
action_plans.gap: Operations bases run a single flat Wi-Fi network shared by guests, corporate devices and some maintenance tablets using a static pre-shared key that has not been rotated in years, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.20/A.8.21 network security.
action_plans.remediation: Segment wireless into separate corporate (802.1X/certificate-based), guest (isolated internet-only) and IoT/maintenance SSIDs, migrate corporate Wi-Fi to per-user certificate authentication, decommission the shared PSK, and add rogue-AP detection to the monitoring scope.

action_plans.name: Joiner Provisioning Least-Privilege for New Hires
action_plans.gap: New hires are provisioned by cloning an existing colleague's access, leading to privilege accumulation well beyond role requirements; a sample showed new analysts holding admin rights they never needed, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.2 privileged access and least-privilege.
action_plans.remediation: Replace clone-based provisioning with role-based access profiles defined per job function, drive provisioning from the joiner workflow, require explicit approval for any access beyond the role baseline, and run a one-off entitlement clean-up to strip excess privileges identified in the review.

action_plans.name: Disaster Recovery Test for the Customer Self-Service Portal
action_plans.gap: The customer self-service portal has a documented DR plan but it has never been executed; the recovery procedure references decommissioned infrastructure and the stated RTO is unverified, breaching ISO 27001 A.5.30 ICT readiness for continuity.
action_plans.remediation: Update the portal DR runbook to current architecture, perform a full failover test to the secondary region measuring actual RTO/RPO against targets, remediate gaps found, and schedule annual DR tests with results reported to the BCM committee and management review.

action_plans.name: Secure Software Development Lifecycle for Internal Apps
action_plans.gap: The in-house development team building the customer portal and integration APIs has no secure-coding standard, no SAST/dependency scanning in the pipeline, and no security review gate before release, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.25-A.8.28.
action_plans.remediation: Embed SAST, secret-scanning and software-composition analysis into the CI pipeline with build-breaking thresholds, adopt a secure-coding standard with developer training, require a security review and threat model for new features, and maintain a remediated-findings KPI per release.

action_plans.name: Clock Synchronisation Across OT and Log Sources
action_plans.gap: Log timestamps across firewalls, servers and OT historians are inconsistent because devices sync to different or no time sources, making incident correlation and forensic timelines unreliable, a gap against ISO 27001 A.8.17 clock synchronisation.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy redundant internal NTP servers traceable to a reliable time source, configure all IT and OT devices to synchronise to them, monitor drift centrally with alerting, and validate that SIEM-ingested events carry a consistent UTC timestamp during the next incident-correlation review.

action_plans.name: Vendor Risk Tiering and Due Diligence Programme
action_plans.gap: Suppliers are onboarded with no security risk tiering or due diligence; critical OT and cloud vendors receive the same (absent) scrutiny as low-risk stationery suppliers, breaching ISO 27001 A.5.19 and NIS2 supply-chain risk management.
action_plans.remediation: Implement a supplier security risk-tiering model based on data sensitivity and operational criticality, require tier-appropriate due diligence (questionnaire, evidence review, on-site audit for critical OT vendors), record outcomes in the supplier register, and reassess critical suppliers annually.

action_plans.name: Email Anti-Spoofing with DMARC Enforcement
action_plans.gap: The voltara-energy.com domain publishes SPF and DKIM but DMARC is set to p=none, so spoofed emails impersonating executives are delivered; a CEO-fraud attempt nearly succeeded, indicating an email-security gap under ISO 27001 A.8.20.
action_plans.remediation: Move DMARC to p=quarantine then p=reject after monitoring aggregate reports, ensure SPF and DKIM cover all legitimate sending sources including the marketing and ticketing platforms, enable inbound anti-spoofing and impersonation protection, and add executive-impersonation training to the phishing programme.

action_plans.name: Data Protection Impact Assessment for Workforce Geolocation
action_plans.gap: The field-workforce mobile app continuously tracks engineer GPS location during and arguably beyond working hours, but no DPIA was conducted and works-council/employee transparency obligations under GDPR Articles 35 and 88 are unmet.
action_plans.remediation: Conduct a DPIA on workforce geolocation with the DPO, restrict tracking to working hours and a defined operational purpose, disable background tracking, consult the works council (CSE), update the employee privacy notice, and set a short retention period for location history with automated deletion.

action_plans.name: Logging of Privileged Administrator Actions
action_plans.gap: Actions performed by domain, database and cloud administrators are not separately logged or reviewed; a privileged account could make undetected changes, breaching ISO 27001 A.8.15/A.8.18 and the privileged-utility-programs control.
action_plans.remediation: Route privileged sessions through a privileged-access-management gateway with full session recording and command logging, forward privileged-activity logs to the SIEM with tamper-evident storage, build alerts for high-risk admin actions, and institute a monthly privileged-activity review by a peer reviewer.

action_plans.name: ISMS Scope and Statement of Applicability Accuracy
action_plans.gap: The ISMS scope statement omits the recently acquired hydro portfolio and the trading desk, and the Statement of Applicability lists controls marked applicable with no implementation evidence, breaching ISO 27001 Clauses 4.3 and 6.1.3 and Annex A justification requirements.
action_plans.remediation: Revise the ISMS scope to explicitly include the hydro assets and trading operations, rebuild the SoA mapping each control to its justification, implementation status and evidence, reconcile against the risk-treatment plan, and obtain management approval before the surveillance audit.

action_plans.name: Anti-DDoS Protection for the Public-Facing Portal
action_plans.gap: The customer portal and public website have no DDoS mitigation; a volumetric attack against a peer operator highlighted that Voltara's edge would saturate, with no detection or scrubbing capability, a resilience gap under ISO 27001 A.8.20/A.5.30.
action_plans.remediation: Front internet-facing services with a cloud DDoS-protection and WAF service providing volumetric and application-layer mitigation, define rate-limiting and geo/bot rules, establish an attack-response runbook with the provider, and validate protection through a coordinated stress test.

action_plans.name: Secure Disposal of Decommissioned Storage Media
action_plans.gap: Decommissioned servers and disks from a datacentre refresh were sent to a general recycler without certified data destruction or asset tracking, risking exposure of residual data, breaching ISO 27001 A.7.14 secure disposal of equipment.
action_plans.remediation: Engage a certified ITAD provider issuing per-asset destruction certificates, maintain a chain-of-custody log from removal to destruction, enforce cryptographic erasure or physical shredding by media type, and reconcile destroyed assets against the CMDB to formally retire each record.

action_plans.name: Identity Federation and SSO for SaaS Sprawl
action_plans.gap: Roughly two dozen SaaS applications use local username/password accounts outside the identity provider, with no central deprovisioning or MFA, so leavers retain access and password reuse is rampant, breaching ISO 27001 A.5.16/A.5.17.
action_plans.remediation: Onboard all sanctioned SaaS applications to SSO via the IdP with enforced MFA, drive automated deprovisioning from the leaver workflow, run a discovery exercise to find shadow SaaS via CASB, and migrate or sunset apps that cannot federate within a defined deadline.

action_plans.name: Risk Assessment Methodology Calibration (EBIOS RM)
action_plans.gap: The EBIOS RM risk study uses an undocumented likelihood/impact scale applied inconsistently between analysts, producing non-comparable risk levels; this undermines the risk-assessment process required by ISO 27001 Clause 6.1.2.
action_plans.remediation: Document a calibrated EBIOS RM scale with explicit likelihood and impact anchors and worked examples, retrain analysts and run a calibration workshop on a common scenario, re-score the existing risk register against the calibrated scale, and add periodic peer review of risk ratings.

action_plans.name: Web Application Firewall Tuning and Coverage
action_plans.gap: The WAF in front of the customer portal runs in detection-only mode with default rules and many false positives suppressed wholesale, so real attacks pass through unblocked, a gap against ISO 27001 A.8.26 application security requirements.
action_plans.remediation: Move the WAF to blocking mode after a tuned learning period, replace blanket suppressions with targeted exclusions, enable the OWASP core rule set with virtual-patching for known CVEs, and review WAF alerts in the SOC with a monthly false-positive and blocked-attack report.

action_plans.name: Personal Data Breach Register and Notification Readiness
action_plans.gap: Voltara keeps no internal breach register and has no documented assessment process to decide on 72-hour CNIL notification and data-subject communication, so a recent minor data leak was neither logged nor risk-assessed, breaching GDPR Articles 33 and 34.
action_plans.remediation: Establish an internal personal-data-breach register, define a severity-assessment and notification-decision procedure aligned to the 72-hour CNIL deadline, integrate it with the security incident process, pre-draft notification templates, and run a breach simulation with the DPO and legal.

action_plans.name: Maintenance and Spare-Parts Integrity for Control Systems
action_plans.gap: Spare protection relays and RTUs held for the substations are stored unsealed in an open store-room with no integrity verification before installation, creating a hardware supply-chain tampering risk uncovered by ISO 27001 A.7.13/A.8.30 controls.
action_plans.remediation: Store critical OT spares in a tamper-evident, access-controlled cabinet, verify firmware integrity and authenticity (checksums/signatures) before field installation, source spares only through authorised channels with provenance records, and add spare-parts integrity checks to the maintenance procedure.

action_plans.name: Acceptable Use and Internet Filtering Policy Enforcement
action_plans.gap: There is no acceptable-use policy acknowledgement and no web-filtering, so staff reach known malware and phishing domains freely; an infection originated from a malvertising site, indicating a gap against ISO 27001 A.5.10 and A.8.23 web filtering.
action_plans.remediation: Deploy DNS-layer and proxy web filtering blocking malicious and high-risk categories, publish an acceptable-use policy with mandatory annual acknowledgement, log and alert on policy-violating access, and align filtering categories with the data-classification and awareness programmes.

action_plans.name: Continuity of Knowledge and Single-Person Dependency in OT
action_plans.gap: Critical knowledge of the hydro plant control-system configuration rests with a single soon-to-retire engineer, with no documentation or succession plan, creating an availability and continuity risk against ISO 27001 A.5.2 roles and A.5.30 readiness.
action_plans.remediation: Document the hydro control-system architecture, configurations and runbooks in the controlled knowledge base, cross-train at least two additional engineers, define backup-role assignments for critical OT functions, and capture the retiring engineer's tacit knowledge through structured handover sessions before departure.

action_plans.name: API Security and Rate Limiting for Partner Integrations
action_plans.gap: The integration APIs shared with aggregators and grid partners use long-lived static API keys, lack rate limiting and have no input validation logging, exposing them to abuse and data scraping, a gap against ISO 27001 A.8.26 and A.8.28.
action_plans.remediation: Replace static keys with short-lived OAuth tokens scoped per partner, enforce per-client rate limiting and quotas at the API gateway, add schema validation and anomaly logging, publish an API security standard for partner onboarding, and rotate or revoke all legacy keys after migration.

action_plans.name: Environmental Monitoring for the On-Premise Server Room
action_plans.gap: The Lyon HQ server room has no temperature, humidity, water or smoke monitoring linked to alerting; a cooling failure over a weekend caused thermal shutdowns that went unnoticed, breaching ISO 27001 A.7.5/A.7.8 supporting-utilities controls.
action_plans.remediation: Install environmental sensors (temperature, humidity, leak, smoke) with 24/7 alerting to the NOC, add redundant cooling and UPS with monitored runtime, document an environmental-alarm response runbook, and include the server room in the periodic physical-security and facilities review.

swots.name: SWOT 2026 - Overall Information Security Posture (ISO 27001 ISMS)

items.description: ISO 27001:2022 certification maintained since 2023 with zero major nonconformities at the last surveillance audit, giving the Lyon-headquartered ISMS a mature, externally validated baseline across the corporate IT estate.

Executive-level sponsorship is concrete: the CISO reports directly to the COO and security KPIs are a standing agenda item at the monthly Voltara executive committee, ensuring funding and rapid decision-making for the security roadmap.

The ISMS scope still excludes most operational technology at wind and hydro sites, leaving a large fraction of Voltara's actual risk surface outside the certified perimeter and unmanaged by the corporate control set.

Security headcount is thin for the asset footprint: a five-person central team supports 40+ generation sites across France, Spain and Germany, creating single-points-of-knowledge and chronic backlog on control implementation.

The 2022 revision of ISO 27001 (Annex A consolidated into 93 controls with new themes like threat intelligence and cloud security) lets Voltara modernise its Statement of Applicability and align it cleanly with NIS2 and GDPR in a single control framework.
Rising customer and lender demand for demonstrable security maturity in green-energy PPAs makes the certification a commercial differentiator when bidding for corporate renewable supply contracts.

Energy operators are explicitly targeted by state-aligned threat actors (e.g. Sandworm-style campaigns against European grid infrastructure), raising the baseline likelihood of a sophisticated, well-resourced attack regardless of Voltara's control maturity.

Overlapping regulatory regimes (ISO 27001, NIS2, GDPR, plus French ANSSI guidance) risk audit fatigue and conflicting evidence requests that could divert the small security team from genuine risk reduction toward documentation churn.

strategies.description: Leverage the validated ISO 27001 ISMS and executive sponsorship to formalise the certification as a contractual security assurance in corporate PPA bids, converting compliance maturity into commercial advantage in the renewable supply market.

Use the mature ISMS governance and exec committee visibility to fund a dedicated threat-intelligence capability (new Annex A 5.7 control) focused on energy-sector adversaries, hardening the corporate estate against targeted state-aligned campaigns.

Adopt the consolidated 2022 control set as a single mapped framework covering ISO 27001, NIS2 and GDPR so the under-resourced team produces evidence once and reuses it, reducing duplication while progressively extending scope toward OT.

Prioritise a phased extension of the ISMS scope to cover the highest-criticality OT sites first, supported by a co-sourcing arrangement with a specialist MSSP to compensate for limited internal headcount before regulators or attackers exploit the gap.

swots.name: SWOT 2026 - OT and Industrial Control System Security (Wind, Solar, Hydro)

items.description: Voltara's newer onshore wind farms (commissioned post-2021) ship with vendor SCADA segmented behind dedicated firewalls and an IEC 62443 zone-and-conduit design, giving a modern security architecture on roughly a third of the generation fleet.

Field engineering teams maintain detailed, up-to-date asset inventories of turbine controllers, inverters and hydro PLCs in the maintenance CMMS, providing a reliable foundation for OT vulnerability and patch management.

Legacy hydro plants run end-of-life PLCs and HMIs (Windows 7 engineering workstations, unpatchable controllers) that cannot be patched without recertification, leaving exploitable systems exposed for years.
Remote vendor maintenance access to turbine OEM SCADA frequently bypasses the corporate VPN via OEM cellular routers, creating uncontrolled and largely unmonitored ingress paths into the OT network.

Deploying an OT-aware network detection platform (e.g. passive monitoring of Modbus/DNP3/IEC 104 traffic) would give the SOC its first real visibility into industrial assets without touching fragile control systems.

Scheduled multi-year hydro refurbishment programmes create natural windows to embed security-by-design (modern segmented controllers, secure remote access) into capital projects at marginal incremental cost.

Purpose-built ICS malware targeting electric utilities (Industroyer/CrashOverride lineage and successors manipulating IEC 104 and IEC 61850) could trip protection relays or disconnect generation, causing physical outages and grid penalties.

OT component supply chains are concentrated among a few turbine and inverter OEMs, so a compromise of an OEM's firmware update channel could simultaneously affect many Voltara sites with no independent fallback.

strategies.description: Extend the proven IEC 62443 zone-and-conduit model from the modern wind fleet across all sites, using the accurate CMMS asset inventory to scope segmentation and prioritise the riskiest conduits first.

Capitalise on the segmented modern SCADA and strong asset visibility to deploy passive OT network detection tuned for ICS malware behaviours (rogue IEC 104 commands), giving early warning of Industroyer-class attacks before they reach protection relays.

Use the planned hydro refurbishment windows to retire end-of-life PLCs and HMIs and replace uncontrolled OEM cellular access with a brokered, logged secure-remote-access gateway, closing the worst legacy and access gaps as capital is already committed.

Impose contractual firmware-integrity and SBOM requirements on OEMs while compensating for unpatchable legacy systems with virtual patching and strict conduit firewalling, limiting both supply-chain and end-of-life exposure on critical generation assets.

swots.name: SWOT 2026 - Cloud Migration to AWS and Azure (Data Platform and Trading Systems)

items.description: Voltara has adopted infrastructure-as-code (Terraform) with mandatory peer review and a CI pipeline, so cloud landing zones are deployed consistently with guardrails baked in rather than configured by hand.
A cloud centre of excellence pairs the platform engineering team with a security architect, embedding security review into the migration intake process from the first sprint of each workload.

Identity and access management across AWS and Azure is fragmented: privileged roles are still provisioned manually per account, with inconsistent MFA enforcement and no central just-in-time access for cloud administrators.

Several lift-and-shift workloads (the legacy energy-trading and forecasting applications) were migrated without re-architecting, carrying flat networking and over-broad security groups into the cloud.

Adopting a cloud security posture management (CSPM) tool would give continuous, automated detection of misconfigurations across both providers, replacing periodic manual reviews with real-time drift alerts.

Migration is the moment to consolidate logging into a centralised SIEM with cloud-native telemetry (CloudTrail, Azure Activity Logs), dramatically improving detection coverage over the previous on-premises blind spots.

Cloud misconfiguration remains the dominant cause of data exposure incidents industry-wide; a single public storage bucket or over-permissive role could expose generation and customer data with no perimeter to fall back on.

Concentrating critical workloads with two US hyperscalers raises data-sovereignty and Schrems II concerns for European energy and personal data, exposing Voltara to regulatory and contractual pushback from public-sector counterparties.

strategies.description: Extend the IaC guardrails and cloud CoE review to enforce secure-by-default baselines (private subnets, least-privilege roles) on every new landing zone, so migration velocity does not outrun security controls.

Use the IaC and CoE discipline to codify data-residency and encryption controls (EU regions, customer-managed keys) as mandatory policy-as-code, directly addressing sovereignty concerns while keeping deployments repeatable.

Roll out CSPM and a federated identity platform with central just-in-time privileged access to remediate fragmented IAM and continuously catch the misconfigurations introduced by lift-and-shift workloads.

Re-architect the migrated trading and forecasting applications with proper network segmentation and least-privilege roles, prioritising those holding sensitive data, to shrink the blast radius before a misconfiguration becomes a reportable breach.

swots.name: SWOT 2026 - Third-Party and Supply Chain Risk Management

items.description: Procurement contracts now embed a standard security annex (right-to-audit, breach notification within 72 hours, ISO 27001 or SOC 2 evidence) for all new suppliers handling Voltara data or systems.
